title: Chapter 27: Leo's Boundary
content: Dawn broke grey and wet over the marsh, and I was still alive.

I don't know why that surprised me. Perhaps because the night before, swimming up from the paint and the fog and the terrible hungry pull of the canvas, I had been so certain I was dying. The consumption had taken me to the edge—my edges, my face, my self—and I had felt myself blurring into something that was no longer Aurelia. I had been brushstroke. I had been pigment. I had been nearly gone.

But the marsh grass was real beneath my cheek. Cold and damp and real. The mud caked my arms and legs, cracking as I shivered, and my face—my face was still my face, though when I lifted my hand to touch it, my fingertips came away faintly smeared with something that wasn't quite skin. Not quite paint. Something in between.
I heard him before I saw him.

Footsteps on the forest path. Steady. Unhurried. Not the frantic sprint of a rescuer, not the careful creep of someone approaching danger. Just—footsteps. As if he'd known exactly where to find me and had walked there the way you walk to your own kitchen. With belonging.
"Aurelia."
Leo crouched beside me. His eyes moved over me—the mud, the shivering, the strange smeared edges of my face—and I waited for the question. The demand. *What happened? What did you do? Are you okay?*
He didn't ask any of them.

He knelt in the wet grass and slid his arms under me and picked me up like I weighed nothing. Like I was something worth carrying. The mud from my clothes transferred immediately to his flannel shirt, streaking it grey and green, and he didn't flinch. He just adjusted his grip and started walking.
The warmth hit me like a second sunrise.

His chest was broad and solid against my side, and heat radiated through the damp flannel—through the mud—through the rattle in my bones. I pressed my face into his shoulder without thinking, and the smell of him flooded through me. Pine. Wood smoke from a stove that had been burning all night. And beneath those, something baked—bread, maybe, or a pie left cooling on a windowsill. Something warm and made-with-hands. Something that spoke of kitchens and homes and the ordinary world I had nearly forgotten existed.

His footsteps were steady on the forest floor. I could hear each one—the soft compression of earth, the crack of a twig, the whisper of ferns brushing his jeans. A rhythm. A heartbeat of motion. I closed my eyes and let it carry me. Let myself be carried.
It had been so long since anyone had carried me anywhere.

We moved through the trees, and the marsh sounds faded behind us—the gurgle of water, the skitter of crickets—and the forest sounds rose up to replace them. Birdsong, thin and tentative in the pre-dawn. Wind through high branches. The distant conversation of a stream.
And then something else.
A pulse.
Low and deep, somewhere beneath the ground, beneath the sound of Leo's boots, beneath everything. A rhythm I recognized. I opened my eyes.
We were at the Boundary Stones.

Leo set me down gently, and my bare feet touched the moss between the stones. The cold barely registered, because the stones—god, the stones were *alive*. They pulsed with six distinct heartbeats, stronger than I'd ever felt them. Thrum-thrum. Thrum-thrum. Thrum-thrum. The rhythm of six women, six ancestresses, six lines of will and want and refusal stretching back through the centuries. The stones knew me. They sang when I stood among them, and their song was recognition.
But then—
A seventh pulse.
Faint. Barely there. A shadow of a heartbeat, thin as a whisper, but unmistakably present. And it wasn't coming from me.
It was coming from Leo.

He stood very still beside me, his hand still hovering near my elbow from where he'd steadied me. The sunrise was beginning to paint the sky behind the trees—rose gold and pale amber, the first real light I'd seen in days—and in that light, I could see the pulse reflected on his face. Not a glow, exactly. A resonance. As if the stones were answering something in him they'd been waiting to answer for a long time.
"What are you?" I asked.
Not accusing. Not afraid. Wondering. The way you ask a question when the answer might be beautiful instead of terrible.
He stared at the stones. I watched his throat move as he swallowed.

"I don't know." His voice was quiet. Honest. "I just know I've always felt... connected here. Like the forest was waiting for something." He looked down at his hands—broad, capable, flecked with marsh mud from carrying me. "I thought it was me. I think maybe it was you."
The seventh pulse throbbed once, faint but sure, and the six answered it.

We stood there in the thin morning light, the stones breathing around us, and I realized that Leo Hartwell was not what I had thought he was. Not just the safe guy. Not just the steady one. He was part of the mechanism. Another thread in whatever ancient weaving had been done in this valley, stitched into the pattern long before either of us was born.
And he didn't know it any more than I had known, when I first came to Blackwood Vale, what I was.

I sat down between two of the stones. The moss was cold and alive beneath me, and the rain had started again—not hard, just a thin drizzle that misted the air and caught the sunrise and turned everything to silver. Leo sat beside me. Not too close. Close enough.
So I told him.
Everything.

I told him about the curse. About the journals hidden in the walls of Blackwood House, brittle with age and furious with secrets. About the ancestresses—seven generations of Aurelias, each one consumed a little further, each one fighting a little harder, each one leaving behind a record that said *I was here, I was real, I did not go willingly.* I told him about the Hollow Council—those smug, shadowed figures who sat in their chamber beneath the town and administered the contract like it was a deed of sale. About the contract itself, written in something that looked like ink and moved like blood. About the painting.
God, the painting.

I told him how it had nearly taken me. How I had felt myself becoming part of it—pigment and linseed and the flat permanence of a portrait that never changes, never ages, never chooses. How the fog had poured through me like water through a broken dam, filling every hollow with its grey and voiceless certainty. How I had clawed my way out by remembering I had hands. By remembering I could grip. By remembering the feel of a pen between my fingers and the sound of my own name in my own voice.

I told him about Julian. About the gravity of that kiss in the fog—the way the curse had used my want and twisted it into a leash. About how I had wanted him and not-wanted him simultaneously, trapped in the double-bind of a desire that wasn't entirely mine.
I told him all of it, and my voice shook, and the rain kept falling, and the stones kept pulsing their six heartbeats, and the seventh kept time beside them, faint but faithful.
Leo didn't interrupt.

He didn't look away. He didn't tense up or lean back or do any of the things a person does when they're hearing something that doesn't fit in the world they thought they lived in. He just listened. His eyes on my face. His hands loose on his knees. Patient.

When I finished, the silence was enormous. The kind of silence that could become anything—rejection, denial, the slow backing-away of someone who has realized they are sitting next to something impossible.
Leo looked at the stones. Looked at his own hands. Looked at me.
"What do you need?"

The same question he'd asked before. The same question he always asked. The only question that mattered, maybe the only question anyone had ever asked me without embedding a hundred others inside it. Not *what's wrong with you?* Not *how could you?* Not *are you sure?* Just: what do you need.
I stared at him. The rain caught in his eyelashes. The sunrise turned his flannel to gold. The seventh pulse in the stones beat once, soft, as if to say: *yes. This one.*
And I kissed him.

Not because the curse pulled me. I would have known—the fog-logic, the sweet grey gravity, the sense of being moved rather than moving. It wasn't there. The curse was silent. The fog was a hundred miles away, or it was asleep, or it simply could not touch me here, among the stones, in the rain, with this man who asked what I needed.

Not because he rescued me. He hadn't, not really. He'd carried me—yes. He'd brought me here—yes. But I had clawed my own way out of the painting. I had chosen to grip. I had chosen to come back. The kissing was not payment for services rendered.
I kissed him because I chose to.

Because his lips were warm and the rain was cool and the flannel smelled like pine and wood smoke and something baked, something that tasted like a kitchen I might one day stand in. Because his hands came up to frame my face—gently, so gently, as if I were something he was afraid of breaking but not afraid of holding.

Because the taste of him—god—the taste was apples and cinnamon. That's what I thought. Apples and cinnamon, the flavor of a pie I'd never eaten, a home I'd never had, a future I'd never let myself imagine. Warm and spiced and sweet in a way that had nothing to do with enchantment and everything to do with the simple, devastating fact of being wanted by someone who saw you clearly and stayed.
I pulled back. My heart was hammering—not the stones' heartbeat, not the curse's drum, but mine. My own. My own ridiculous, human, choice-made heartbeat.
Leo looked at me. His eyes were the color of the forest floor—brown and green and deep, with flecks of something lighter. Amber, maybe. Sunrise.
He didn't make it dramatic.

He didn't say *I love you* or *I've always* or *you're the most* or any of the things that men say when they are trying to wrap a moment in grandeur. He didn't declare. He didn't promise. He didn't try to catch me in a net of words.
He said: "Okay. So what do we do?"
And something in my chest cracked open—not broken, not wounded, but opened, the way a window opens after a long winter. Air came flooding in.

That was the difference. I saw it clearly for the first time, sitting there in the rain between the pulsing stones. Julian said *I'll save you.* Leo said *What do we do?* One positioned me as damsel. The other positioned me as partner. One was a tower to climb. The other was a door to walk through, side by side.
"What do we do," I repeated. Not a question. A wonder.

"We figure out how to break a curse," I said. "We find the contract. We get seven generations of women to stand together and say they didn't consent. We take on the Hollow Council. And probably we get soaked doing it."
Leo nodded slowly. "Right." He brushed the rain from his forehead. "So. Same as Tuesday, then."

I laughed. It came out cracked and strange and too loud for the quiet morning, and I didn't care. I laughed until my ribs hurt and my eyes stung, and Leo's mouth twitched into that half-smile—the one that wasn't quite a grin, the one he probably didn't know he did, the one that made him look like a boy who'd just discovered that the world contained both danger and pie and wasn't sure which one to be more excited about.

We sat together at the Boundary Stones as the sun rose in earnest. The fog didn't penetrate here—I noticed that now, the way I'd noticed it before but never quite believed. The stones held. Their boundary was real. Beyond the circle, the morning mist curled and thickened, but inside, the air was clean. Clear. As if the world had drawn a breath and held it, giving us this small clean space.

The six heartbeats pulsed steady beneath the moss. The seventh answered—Leo's pulse, whatever it meant, whatever he was, whatever thread the valley had wound through him before he was born. It didn't matter. Not yet. We'd figure it out. *We.*
I pulled my journal from the inner pocket of my ruined jacket—still there, somehow, still dry against my chest where I'd tucked it. The pen was bent but functional. I wrote:
*I kissed a man who asked what I needed.*
*That's the opposite of a curse.*

*A curse tells you what to feel. A choice asks what you want. The fog told me I wanted Julian—it poured that want through me like water through a pipe, and I mistook the flood for thirst. But wanting someone who asks what you need? That's not a flood. That's a well. You draw from it when you're ready. You choose how deep to go.*

*I want to END this. I want to break the contract. I want seven generations of women to stand together—in whatever form they can stand, painting or memory or ghost or girl—and say: WE DID NOT CONSENT. I want the Hollow Council to hear it. I want the valley to remember it.*
*And I want apple pie afterward.*
*Is that too much to ask? A curse broken and a pie eaten and a man beside me who doesn't try to carry me through the whole fight—just through the parts where I'm still shaking?*
*Maybe. Maybe it is too much.*
*But I'm asking anyway.*

I closed the journal. The rain had stopped. The sun was fully up now, and the Boundary Stones glowed faintly in its light—not with magic, exactly, but with the warmth of things that have been waiting in the cold for a very long time and are finally, finally beginning to thaw.
Leo leaned back on his hands and looked at the sky.
"Apple pie," he said, as if this were a perfectly reasonable component of a curse-breaking strategy.
"Apple pie," I confirmed.
"I make a decent one," he said. "My grandmother's recipe."

Of course he did. Of course this man who smelled like wood smoke and baked things and belonged to the stones the way I did—of course he made pie from his grandmother's recipe. The valley had stitched him into this story long before I arrived, and the thread of his belonging was woven right alongside mine.
"Later," I said. "After."
"After," he agreed.

We sat in the quiet and the light, and the stones pulsed their seven heartbeats—not six anymore, and I understood that something had shifted. Not solved. Not won. But shifted, the way a lock shifts when you find the right key. We hadn't broken anything yet. The contract still existed. The Hollow Council still sat in their chamber. The painting still waited in the gallery with its grey and hungry patience.
But I had kissed someone without the curse's permission.
And he had asked what we do, not what he should do.
And somewhere beneath us, six women—seven, if I counted myself—were beginning to drum.

The war was coming. I could feel it in the stones, in the fog that pressed against the boundary but couldn't enter, in the way the sunrise looked like both a promise and a warning—gold and rose and the kind of beauty that makes you brave because you remember it exists. But right now, in this moment, there was only the moss and the rain-washed air and the warmth of another person's shoulder against mine.
Right now, there was peace.

I was going to hold onto it. I was going to hold onto it the way I'd held onto the edge of that painting—gripping, desperate, refusing to let go. Not because I was afraid of losing it. Because I had chosen it.
And choosing was the most dangerous thing of all.
